head_coach.name: Kevin Stefanski
head_coach.coaching_tree: Kubiak/Shanahan wide-zone tree via Minnesota Vikings lineage. Mentored by Brad Childress (Reid/WCO tree), Norv Turner (Air Coryell), Pat Shurmur (traditional WCO), and most influentially Gary Kubiak (Shanahan wide-zone). Two-time NFL Coach of the Year.
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Wide-zone run game married to play-action passing. Run-first identity built from under-center formations with heavy personnel (12, 21, 22 groupings). Play-action creates explosive downfield shots off run-look fakes. Adapts to personnel — used more spread with Watson, reverted to roots with Flacco. Collaborative play-caller who values efficiency, deception, and physical football. Stefanski called plays in Cleveland and is expected to heavily influence or call plays in Atlanta.

offensive_coordinator.name: Tommy Rees
offensive_coordinator.coaching_tree: Notre Dame OC (2020-22) → Alabama OC (2023) → Cleveland Browns TE coach/pass game specialist (2024) → Browns OC (2025) → ATL OC (2026). Young play-caller (33) with college roots and one year of NFL OC experience under Stefanski.
offensive_coordinator.scheme_evolution: Ran a productive passing offense at Notre Dame averaging 30+ PPG in three years as OC. At Alabama, helped Jalen Milroe rank top-5 nationally in yards per attempt. In Cleveland, served as TE coach first (2024) then promoted to OC in 2025 where he worked collaboratively with Stefanski on the offensive system and took over play-calling duties in Week 10. Expected to call plays in Atlanta but within Stefanski's wide-zone framework.
offensive_coordinator.development_track_record: Developed Harold Fannin Jr. to a 72-catch, 731-yard, 6-TD rookie season in Cleveland. As TE coach/pass game specialist, helped David Njoku lead the Browns with 64 catches and 5 TDs in 2024. College track record includes developing multiple QBs at Notre Dame and Alabama. Limited NFL sample but strong TE development pedigree, which aligns perfectly with Kyle Pitts.

defensive_coordinator.name: Jeff Ulbrich
defensive_coordinator.coaching_tree: Pete Carroll/Seattle Seahawks tree (assistant 2010-11) → Dan Quinn/Atlanta Falcons LBs coach (2015-19) → Robert Saleh/NY Jets DC (2021-24). Played 10 years as LB for 49ers. Absorbed Carroll's physicality principles, Quinn's aggressive front philosophy, and Saleh's 4-3 single-high coverage system. Retained by Kevin Stefanski on a new 3-year deal.
defensive_coordinator.scheme_evolution: At Jets, ran a 4-3 defense that was the only unit to rank top-5 in total defense for three consecutive years (2022-24). Core philosophy is front-four pressure with seven in coverage — Jets had NFL's lowest blitz rate in 2022 (16.3%) and 2023 (17.4%). Used significant man coverage (32.5% in NYJ vs 16.4% in ATL pre-Ulbrich). In his first year in Atlanta (2025), shifted the defense dramatically — franchise-record 57 sacks, increased man coverage, more disguised pressures, and a confrontational scheme featuring young edge rushers Pearce and Walker. Emphasizes multiplicity — will show both 3-4 and 4-3 looks.
defensive_coordinator.development_track_record: Elite LB developer: Deion Jones (four 90+ tackle seasons), De'Vondre Campbell (three), Foyesade Oluokun. At UCLA, developed Anthony Barr (led nation in sacks, 1st round), Eric Kendricks (Butkus Award, 2nd round). With Jets, coached Sauce Gardner to first-team All-Pro in first two seasons, developed Quincy Williams into All-Pro LB, Jermaine Johnson to Pro Bowl. In 2025 Atlanta, rookie James Pearce Jr. had 10.5 sacks (best rookie since Parsons), Xavier Watts had 5 INTs, Jalon Walker had 5.5 sacks.

offensive_line_coach.name: Bill Callahan
offensive_line_coach.coaching_tree: One of the NFL's most decorated OL coaches with 48 years of coaching experience. Former Raiders HC (Super Bowl XXXVII). Spent 4 years with Stefanski in Cleveland (2020-23) coaching the Browns' OL to elite rushing production — top-6 in rushing yards every year. Has guided 14 OL to 35 Pro Bowl selections across 27 NFL seasons.
offensive_line_coach.significance: Callahan's presence is a clear signal of Stefanski's commitment to the wide-zone run game. In Cleveland, the Stefanski-Callahan partnership found the ideal balance between wide-zone and gap concepts. His hiring was the first offensive staff move, ahead of the OC — underscoring that the OL and run game are the foundation of this offense.

passing_game_coordinator.name: Tanner Engstrand
passing_game_coordinator.coaching_tree: Spent 5 years with Detroit Lions in various roles, serving as passing game coordinator in 2023-24 under Ben Johnson. Detroit led the NFL in points scored (564), TDs (70), and first downs (410) in 2024. Was Jets OC in 2025 under Aaron Glenn. Brings modern passing concepts from one of the NFL's most prolific offenses to complement Stefanski's run-first foundation.

quarterbacks_coach.name: Alex Van Pelt
quarterbacks_coach.coaching_tree: Longtime NFL QB coach (Bills, Packers, Browns). Was OC on Stefanski's Cleveland staff through 2024 and part of both playoff runs. Reunites with Stefanski in Atlanta. Deep familiarity with Stefanski's system.

offense.scheme_detail: Stefanski's offense is built on the wide-zone run game married to play-action passing — a system he absorbed from Gary Kubiak during their one season together in Minnesota (2019). The foundation is under-center run action that sets up explosive play-action shots downfield. Unlike pure Shanahan-tree systems that live in 11 personnel, Stefanski diversifies heavily into 12, 21, and even 22 personnel groupings to create mismatches and allow heavier run formations. He was the NFL's most under-center, most play-action-heavy, and most run-heavy play-caller during his Minnesota OC tenure. In Cleveland, the Browns were a top-5 rushing team in his first three years. The scheme puts minimal pressure on the QB by creating easy completions off play-action and reducing pure dropback situations. Tommy Rees adds college-influenced passing concepts, and Tanner Engstrand brings Detroit's modern passing game wrinkles, creating a blend of Stefanski's run-first foundation with more sophisticated pass game layers.
offense.run_scheme: Wide-zone primary with gap concepts (power, counter) mixed in. Stefanski loves to pull athletic guards in the run game — guards pulling on power, counter, and pin-and-pull perimeter concepts. The wide-zone stretch play is the bread and butter, designed to force defenses to respect every gap and create cutback lanes. Bill Callahan's presence reinforces this — under his tutelage in Cleveland, the Browns gained 2,374 rushing yards in 2020 (3rd in NFL). Gap concepts provide change-up and short-yardage solutions. With Bijan Robinson, who thrived in wide-zone under previous staff and also has gap-scheme roots from Texas, the run game has an elite foundational talent.
offense.pass_architecture: Play-action passing is the primary pass-game driver. Deep crossing routes, post routes, and bootleg concepts off run-action fakes. Stefanski prefers under-center play-action for maximum deception. Quick-game timing routes (slants, curls, digs) provide the short-to-intermediate layer. TE-centric passing is a hallmark — Stefanski's teams have ranked no lower than 7th in TE targets in six years as Browns HC. Boot concepts give the QB an easy half-field read with a flat/crosser/go progression. Shotgun play-action with guard-pulling has been added as a modern wrinkle. Engstrand's influence from Detroit's offense may add more RPO and spread-concept layers, but the core remains run-action-based.
offense.personnel_tendencies: Heavy personnel groupings are a Stefanski trademark. In Minnesota (2019), he ranked 1st in 21 personnel usage, 1st in 22 personnel, and last in 11 personnel. In Cleveland with Baker Mayfield, moved to more 12 personnel looks. Expects significant 12 personnel usage in Atlanta with Kyle Pitts (franchise-tagged) and potentially a second TE. 11 personnel for passing downs, 12 and 21 for base offense. The diversity of groupings is a weapon — it forces defenses to match personnel rather than sitting in nickel all game. Fullback or H-back usage is possible given Stefanski's history with 21/22 looks.
offense.motion_tempo: Moderate-to-high motion usage. Stefanski uses jet motion, orbit motion, and TE trades to create pre-snap conflict and reveal coverage. Tempo is deliberate — this is a ball-control offense that wants to control the clock and keep drives alive. Not a hurry-up system. Misdirection through play-action, personnel groupings, and varied presentations is more central than motion alone.

defense.scheme_detail: Ulbrich's defense is rooted in 4-3 principles from his time under Robert Saleh (who came from the Pete Carroll/Seattle tree) but he explicitly emphasizes multiplicity — he will show both 4-3 and 3-4 looks depending on personnel and situation. In his first year in Atlanta (2025), the defense produced a franchise-record 57 sacks (2nd in NFL) by unleashing young edge rushers James Pearce Jr. and Jalon Walker. The core philosophy is violence, physicality, and relentless effort. Ulbrich wants his front four to generate pressure without needing to blitz, allowing seven defenders in coverage. When healthy and talented, this is an extremely low-blitz defense (16-17% blitz rate at Jets). However, in Atlanta, he's adapted — blitzing more frequently to maximize the talents of his versatile young pass rushers, particularly Walker's hybrid LB/EDGE skillset.
defense.front_structure: Multiple front using 4-3 base nomenclature. Two DEs with hand in dirt, a nose/DT combination inside, and EDGE rushers who can align standing up or in 3-point stance. Pearce operates primarily as a speed-rush DE, while Walker moves between EDGE and off-ball LB. Ruke Orhorhoro at DT provides interior pass-rush juice (ranked 3rd among DTs in QB pressure rate in 2025). The front rotates personnel aggressively to keep pass rushers fresh. In sub-packages, can shift to odd-front looks with a single nose and two wide-alignment EDGEs.
defense.coverage_philosophy: Balanced man/zone blend — a significant departure from pre-Ulbrich Atlanta. At the Jets, Ulbrich ran man coverage on 32.5% of snaps (8th most in NFL) compared to the Falcons' 16.4% under Jimmy Lake. Cover 1 (man-free) is a primary call, used ~28% of the time at NYJ. Cover 3 and Cover 4 (quarters) provide zone variety. In 2025 Atlanta, the scheme shifted to more man coverage and disguised pressures, with A.J. Terrell's return enabling island coverage on one side. The defense emphasizes limiting big plays — at NYJ, the 2022 Jets were the only defense to prevent a 300-yard passer all season. Pattern-matching zone concepts (quarters) allow DBs to play man-like technique within zone principles.
defense.pressure_philosophy: Front-four pressure is the ideal. Ulbrich's Jets had the NFL's lowest blitz rate in 2022 and 2023 yet generated elite pressure. The philosophy is: invest in DL talent, let them win 1-on-1, drop seven in coverage. However, Ulbrich adapted in 2025 Atlanta — blitzing more frequently to compensate for still-developing DL depth while maximizing Walker's versatility as a blitzer from multiple alignments. Simulated pressures and mugged-up LB looks (A-gap mug, edge-to-LB stunts) create confusion without sending extra rushers. The ideal end-state is four-man pressure dominance with coverage variety behind it.
defense.sub_packages: Nickel (~70% of snaps) is the primary sub-package — Ulbrich operated in nickel 70.1% of the time at NYJ. Base 4-3 (~27%) for heavy personnel. Very little dime (1.8% at NYJ vs 7.1% ATL pre-Ulbrich). The nickel defender in Ulbrich's system has been a slot CB type (Micheal Carter II at NYJ), not a safety hybrid. Billy Bowman Jr. filled a similar CB/nickel role in 2025 Atlanta. This personnel philosophy means Ulbrich prefers to defend 11 personnel with nickel rather than going lighter to dime, trusting his LBs and front to handle the extra gaps.

QB.scheme_role: The QB in Stefanski's offense operates primarily from under center, executing run fakes and play-action boots that set up intermediate-to-deep shots. He must process half-field reads off play-action and deliver accurate timing throws on West Coast concepts. The scheme minimizes the number of difficult throws by creating easy completions off misdirection, but the QB must be disciplined in footwork and progressions. Pre-snap diagnosis of coverage is important for audibles at the line, particularly in 12 personnel where the defense's response to heavy personnel reveals coverage.

Arm Strength.reasoning: Stefanski's play-action shots go deep off crossing routes and post patterns. The QB needs to drive the ball downfield off bootleg platforms and fit throws into tight windows on deep crossers. Not a prerequisite for the quick game, but essential for the explosive plays that make this offense dangerous.

Accuracy.reasoning: The foundation of Stefanski's West Coast/Kubiak passing game is timing. The QB must deliver accurately on boots, rollouts, and from the pocket on slants, curls, and digs. Anticipation throws — delivering the ball before the receiver completes the break — are critical. Kirk Cousins thrived here precisely because of his accuracy on play-action.

Pocket Presence.reasoning: Under-center play-action requires the QB to sell run fakes with his back to the defense, then reset and find receivers. He must navigate a moving pocket, step up into lanes, and deliver from varied platforms (boot, half-roll, straight drop). Joe Flacco succeeded in this system despite limited mobility because his pocket feel was elite.

Mobility.reasoning: Stefanski uses boot concepts extensively, requiring the QB to execute on the move. This isn't about scrambling — it's about executing designed movement passes. Some mobility is necessary to sell fakes and get to boot spots. Stefanski has succeeded with both mobile (Dalvin Cook/Cousins RPOs) and immobile (Flacco) QBs, but mobility adds a dimension.

Decision Making.reasoning: Play-action presents half-field reads with clear progressions (flat-crosser-go on boots), but the QB must process quickly because the defense can recover from run fakes. Poor decisions — forcing throws into coverage, holding the ball too long — collapse the scheme's advantage. Stefanski's system generates easy reads but punishes late decisions.

Leadership.reasoning: Stefanski values stable, even-keeled QBs (Cousins, Flacco types) who manage the game and lead the huddle. This is a team-first offense where the QB's ego must be secondary to the run game's dominance. Leadership matters but is less schematically driven than other traits.

Pre-Snap Diagnosis.reasoning: Heavy personnel formations create pre-snap diagnostic opportunities — how the defense aligns to 12 or 21 personnel reveals coverage and run-fit tendencies. The QB must check into optimal plays at the line. Stefanski's system has built-in RPO-like checks that require pre-snap reads, particularly in heavy sets.

QB.ideal_archetype: A pocket-comfortable, accurate passer who thrives in play-action and delivers on timing routes from under center. Not a pure gunslinger or a scrambler, but a disciplined, smart QB who can execute on the move when boot concepts call for it. Processes half-field reads efficiently and rarely puts the ball in danger. Think Kirk Cousins in his 2019 Vikings peak or Joe Flacco's late-2023 Browns run — QBs who let the scheme work for them rather than trying to do too much.

height.reasoning: Under-center snaps, play fakes with back to defense, and throwing over OL on timing routes all benefit from height. Stefanski has historically preferred taller QBs (Cousins 6-3, Flacco 6-6, Mayfield 6-1 was his shortest).

weight.reasoning: Physicality matters for selling run fakes and absorbing hits on boot concepts when the backside DE doesn't bite. Not a strict threshold but heavier QBs handle the under-center game better.

QB.development_context: Stefanski has a strong track record of developing QBs within his system. He took over as interim OC in Minnesota and Kirk Cousins immediately improved. Baker Mayfield had his best season in 2020 under Stefanski. Joe Flacco came off the street and played MVP-caliber football. The system is QB-friendly by design — it creates easy reads and protects the passer with the run game. A young QB who arrives with accuracy and pocket feel can be developed within the system; arm talent and pre-snap processing can be refined. Van Pelt's presence as QB coach further reinforces the development pipeline.
QB.scheme_fit_narrative: This offense is designed to make the QB's job easier, not harder. We need a guy who can stand in the pocket, sell play-action, and deliver accurate balls on timing routes. He doesn't need to be a magician — he needs to be efficient, protect the football, and let Bijan Robinson and the run game do the heavy lifting. The explosive plays come from the scheme, not from the QB's arm.

RB.scheme_role: The RB is the engine of Stefanski's offense. He must excel in wide-zone concepts, reading the front side and cutback lanes created by the OL's lateral displacement. He carries the ball 20+ times per game and is the primary weapon for controlling tempo. Pass-catching is important in 12 personnel — the RB is often the checkdown or a route-runner out of the backfield on play-action. Pass protection is necessary to keep the QB upright on drop-back downs. Bijan Robinson is already the ideal — this blueprint describes what a backup or future replacement would need.

Vision.reasoning: Wide-zone demands elite vision. The RB must read the flow of blocks, identify the front-side crease or cutback lane, and make decisive cuts. The stretch play doesn't always create obvious holes — vision is what separates good from great in this scheme. Nick Chubb's vision made Cleveland's run game elite.

Contact Balance.reasoning: Wide-zone doesn't always create clean lanes. The RB often makes first contact at or near the line of scrimmage and must stay on his feet through arm tackles to get to the second level. Bijan Robinson's contact balance is a model for what this scheme demands.

Power.reasoning: Gap concepts (power, counter) mixed into the run game require some interior power, but this is secondary to zone-running traits. The RB needs enough power to finish runs between the tackles but isn't asked to be a pure downhill pounder.

Elusiveness.reasoning: The cutback course in wide zone requires lateral agility and the ability to make defenders miss in space. When the stretch side is walled off, the RB must plant and explode through the backside. Elusiveness at the second level turns 5-yard runs into 15-yard gains.

Speed.reasoning: Wide-zone stretch plays require the RB to threaten the edge and force the defense to flow horizontally. Breakaway speed turns cutbacks into explosive plays. Stefanski's offenses have always had home-run capability (Dalvin Cook, Chubb, Robinson).

Pass Catching.reasoning: Stefanski uses the RB as a receiver out of the backfield on play-action concepts, screens, and check-downs. Robinson is a 50+ catch-per-season back. The RB must be a reliable target to keep the offense efficient on passing downs and give the QB a safety valve.

Pass Protection.reasoning: The RB must recognize blitzers and pick up free rushers on passing downs. Stefanski's under-center play-action can leave the RB responsible for the backside DE if the fake doesn't hold. Not the primary trait but necessary for staying on the field in all situations.

RB.ideal_archetype: A complete, zone-running back who can read blocks laterally, make decisive cuts, and explode through cutback lanes. Must have the speed to threaten the edge on stretch plays and the receiving ability to be a weapon in the pass game. Think Dalvin Cook in Minnesota or Nick Chubb in Cleveland — backs who made Stefanski's wide-zone scheme sing. Bijan Robinson is literally the ideal; a backup must share his zone-running instincts even if lacking his overall talent.

weight.reasoning: Wide-zone backs need enough mass to absorb contact at the LOS but enough speed to stretch the field. Stefanski has preferred mid-range backs (Chubb 227, Cook 210, Robinson 215) who can handle volume.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: The stretch play requires speed to threaten the edge. Backs who can't force the defense to over-pursue horizontally limit the cutback lanes that make this scheme explosive.

short_shuttle.reasoning: Lateral agility is critical for reading wide-zone blocks and making the cut from stretch course to cutback. The shuffle-cut in zone running demands elite lateral quickness.

RB.development_context: Stefanski has a track record of maximizing RB talent. Dalvin Cook had his best seasons under Stefanski. Nick Chubb flourished. Even undrafted/later picks like D'Ernest Johnson were productive. The scheme does a lot of the work — the OL creates lanes, the play-action freezes defenders. What must arrive is vision and lateral quickness in zone concepts. Pass-catching can be developed. Michael Pitre, retained as RBs coach, has continuity with Bijan Robinson from the previous staff.
RB.scheme_fit_narrative: We need a zone-running back who can make one cut and go. The OL is going to stretch the defense horizontally — the back needs to read it, find the crease, and hit it with speed. He's going to carry the ball a lot and catch passes out of the backfield. Physical toughness to handle a heavy workload is non-negotiable. This isn't a committee — we need a lead back.

WR.scheme_role: WRs in Stefanski's offense operate in a complementary role to the run game and TE-centric passing attack. The outside WR must win on play-action deep shots (posts, crosses, go routes off boot looks) and be reliable on intermediate timing routes (curls, digs, comebacks). In 12 personnel, there's often only one WR on the field, meaning he must be versatile enough to beat man coverage and win contested situations. Drake London is the WR1 archetype — a big-bodied, contested-catch WR who can also play power slot. WR2/WR3 may need more speed to stretch the field vertically and create space for the TE and RB underneath.

Route Running.reasoning: Timing routes are the backbone of Stefanski's passing game. The WR must run precise routes at exact depths to allow the QB's footwork to dictate throw timing. Amari Cooper thrived in Cleveland because of his route-running precision.

Separation.reasoning: Play-action naturally creates separation by freezing defenders. The scheme doesn't demand elite separation the way a spread offense would, but the WR still needs to create windows against man coverage when the Falcons are in 11 personnel passing downs.

Hands.reasoning: Play-action shots create tight-window throws where the ball arrives with defensive backs closing. The WR must make difficult catches in traffic. Drake London's strong hands are the model — this offense generates high-difficulty catch opportunities more than wide-open layups.

YAC Ability.reasoning: Stefanski's quick-game concepts (slants, crossers) can create YAC opportunities. The WR needs to be dangerous after the catch but YAC is secondary to route precision and contested-catch ability in this scheme. Deep crossers off play-action can become big YAC plays.

Speed.reasoning: The play-action deep shot is Stefanski's kill shot — the defense bites on run action and the WR runs past for a 40+ yard completion. At least one WR on the roster must have the speed to take the top off the defense and keep safeties honest. Without vertical threat, the play-action game loses its most explosive element.

Contested Catches.reasoning: Heavy personnel groupings mean the WR is often in 1-on-1 situations against a team's best CB. Play-action creates deep-shot opportunities where the ball arrives in tight windows. Drake London's contested-catch ability (top-5 in the NFL) is tailor-made for this. The ideal WR is big enough to win at the catch point.

Release Package.reasoning: When the Falcons face man coverage, the WR must be able to release from press. Ulbrich's defense uses man coverage ~30% of the time — Stefanski's offense will face similar man rates. A competent release package is necessary but not the defining trait, as play-action and motion often create free releases.

WR.ideal_archetype: A big, physical WR who can win contested catches on deep play-action shots and be precise on intermediate timing routes. Think a Drake London or Amari Cooper type — not a pure speedster, but a complete receiver who can win in contested situations, run precise routes, and handle being the solo WR in heavy personnel groupings. A complementary WR2 should be a speed threat who stretches the field vertically.

height.reasoning: Stefanski's offense creates contested-catch opportunities where height and catch radius matter. Bigger WRs also help in the run game as blockers, which is important in a run-first scheme. London (6-4), Cooper (6-1) fit this profile.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Must be fast enough to threaten deep off play-action. The WR2/WR3 may need sub-4.45 speed to be the vertical threat that opens up the intermediate game underneath.

WR.development_context: Robert Prince, hired from Miami as WR coach, brings experience developing WR talent. Stefanski's offense doesn't demand elite route-running precision from Day 1 — play-action creates natural separation. What must arrive is hands, contested-catch ability, and speed. Route technique can be refined within the timing structure. The scheme's simplicity for WRs (run precise routes at exact depths, sell play-action blocks on run plays) means young WRs can contribute early.
WR.scheme_fit_narrative: Our WR1 needs to be a big, physical target who can win 50-50 balls on play-action shots and run precise timing routes underneath. He's going to be the only receiver on the field in 12 personnel a lot, so he needs to be trustworthy in every situation. The WR2 is our speed element — the guy who takes the top off and makes the safety stay deep so the TE and RB can work underneath.

TE.scheme_role: The TE is arguably the most important skill position in Stefanski's offense after the RB. Stefanski has ranked no lower than 7th in TE targets in six years as HC. The TE operates as both an inline blocker in 12 personnel run game (creating extra gaps for wide-zone) and a primary receiving weapon in play-action. He must be capable of flexing into the slot and running a full route tree — seam routes, crossers, and angle routes off play-action are staples. Kyle Pitts (franchise-tagged) is the receiving TE; Stefanski's system will likely also feature a second, more blocking-oriented TE in 12 personnel.

Receiving.reasoning: Stefanski's TEs are featured in the passing game at an elite rate. David Njoku averaged 60+ targets per year. Harold Fannin Jr. had 72 catches as a rookie. The TE must be a reliable receiver who can be a primary target in the progression, not just a check-down. Kyle Pitts' 88-catch, 928-yard 2025 season shows the upside.

Route Running.reasoning: The TE runs a diverse route tree in this scheme — seams, crossers, angle routes, out-breaking routes off boot action. He must be able to find soft spots in zone coverage and separate against LBs in man. The routes aren't simple flat/seam — they require intermediate route-running ability.

Blocking.reasoning: In 12 personnel wide-zone, the TE is an essential blocker. He must seal the backside DE on stretch plays, kick out at the point of attack on gap schemes, and sustain blocks at the second level. This is where Stefanski's offense differs from spread TE usage — the TE must legitimately block. The Falcoholic noted that 'a tight end that brings a bit more oomph as a run blocker would be a better fit' with Stefanski's physical identity.

Athleticism.reasoning: The TE must be athletic enough to flex into the slot and win on seam routes against safeties. In Stefanski's system, the TE is often the primary threat on play-action — he needs the athleticism to separate at the intermediate level and create mismatch problems. Pitts' freakish athleticism is the ceiling.

Hands.reasoning: Play-action throws to TEs often come in traffic between LBs and safeties. The TE must secure contested catches at the intermediate level. Drops in the middle of the field kill drives in a ball-control offense.

Speed.reasoning: Seam routes off play-action require enough speed to threaten the safety downfield and prevent them from jumping short routes. Not asking for WR speed, but the TE needs to be fast enough to stretch the field vertically when aligned in the slot.

TE.ideal_archetype: A complete TE who can both block in 12 personnel wide-zone and be a featured receiver in the play-action passing game. Must be athletic enough to flex to the slot and create mismatches against LBs and safeties. Think David Njoku or Travis Kelce (in terms of versatility, not just receiving) — a TE who makes the defense account for him in both phases. Pitts represents the receiving-first version; a second TE might be the blocking-first version (think Harrison Bryant in Cleveland).

height.reasoning: Size matters for sealing blocks on wide-zone and winning at the catch point on seam routes. Stefanski's TEs (Njoku 6-4, Pitts 6-6, Fannin 6-4) have been big-bodied.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to sustain blocks at the POA in 12 personnel run game. Lighter TEs who can't block limit the offense's ability to use heavy formations effectively.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: The TE needs enough speed to threaten on seam routes and create separation after the catch. Sub-4.70 preferred for the receiving TE role. The blocking TE can be slower.

TE.development_context: This coaching staff has an elite TE development pedigree. Stefanski coached TEs in Minnesota. Rees was Cleveland's TE coach before becoming OC and developed Harold Fannin Jr. to a 72-catch rookie season. Kevin Koger, retained as TE coach, has worked with Pitts for two years. If any position can be developed by this staff, it's TE. A raw athletic TE prospect with blocking deficiencies can be developed into a complete player. Receiving technique can be refined. What must arrive is athleticism and willingness to block.
TE.scheme_fit_narrative: The TE is going to be the second-most targeted player in this offense. He needs to be able to block on early downs in 12 personnel and be a featured receiver in the play-action game. We want a guy who defenses have to account for in both phases — not just a big WR who can't block, and not just a blocker who catches a few balls. The ideal is a true Y TE who can do everything.

OT.scheme_role: OTs in Stefanski's wide-zone scheme must be athletic enough to execute reach blocks, seal the backside on stretch plays, and climb to the second level to engage LBs. In pass protection, the OT must handle speed rushers off the edge on play-action boots (the backside OT often faces an unblocked DE on boot concepts and must sell the run fake before recovering). Under-center play-action places unique demands — the OT must fire off the ball as if run blocking before transitioning to pass sets. Bill Callahan's presence means elite OL coaching with specific technique demands.

Pass Protection.reasoning: Play-action generates hits. The OT must protect the QB on drop-back passing downs and recover from run-fake sells to pass sets. The backside OT on boot concepts has a particularly difficult assignment. Callahan's OL units in Cleveland allowed the 2nd-fewest pressures in 2020 despite a run-first scheme.

Run Blocking.reasoning: Wide-zone is the foundation. The OT must execute reach blocks (getting lateral displacement to seal the edge), seal the backside (preventing penetration that kills cutback lanes), and climb to the second level. This is THE defining trait — Callahan and Stefanski's run game cannot function without OTs who can move laterally.

Footwork.reasoning: Wide-zone footwork is specific — the OT must take a lateral first step and sustain lateral movement while maintaining leverage. Sloppy feet mean losing the reach block and killing the stretch play. Callahan is renowned for drilling precise footwork technique. This is more important here than in gap/power schemes.

Anchor.reasoning: Must hold up against bull-rushes on pass protection downs. Not the defining trait because the scheme features quick play-action that reduces pass-rush exposure, but the OT still needs to anchor when the pocket is required for downfield shots.

Athleticism.reasoning: Wide-zone OTs must move. They must get lateral displacement on the first step, reach-block DEs who are aligning wide, and climb to the second level in space. A stiff OT who can only pass-set kills this run scheme. This is why Stefanski values athletic OL — Jack Conklin earned All-Pro in 2020 because of his ability to move in space.

Strength.reasoning: Baseline strength to sustain blocks and hold the point of attack. Not the premium trait because wide-zone is more about positioning and angles than raw power at the POA. Gap concepts require more strength, but they're secondary in this scheme.

OT.ideal_archetype: An athletic, mobile OT who excels at reach-blocking in wide-zone and has the footwork to climb to the second level. Must be able to transition from run-blocking to pass-protection seamlessly on play-action. Think Jack Conklin in 2020 Cleveland or Jedrick Wills Jr. at his best — OTs who can move laterally and sustain blocks in space. The modern zone-blocking OT, not a pure phone-booth mauler.

short_shuttle.reasoning: Wide-zone requires lateral agility to execute reach blocks and sustain lateral movement. The short shuttle directly measures the lateral burst needed for the first step in zone blocking.

3_cone_drill.reasoning: Change-of-direction ability for climbing to the second level and adjusting to defensive movement in space.

height.reasoning: Length helps with reach-blocking angles and pass protection. The current Falcons OL (Matthews 6-5, Bergeron 6-5) is tall.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to sustain blocks but not so heavy that lateral quickness is compromised. Callahan has developed OTs in the 305-325 range.

OT.development_context: Bill Callahan is among the NFL's best OL developers. He coached Jack Conklin to All-Pro, Joel Bitonio to multiple All-Pros, and developed Wyatt Teller into an elite guard. Callahan can refine technique at an elite level — particularly wide-zone footwork. A raw athletic OT with poor technique but good movement skills can be transformed under Callahan. What must arrive is natural athleticism and lateral movement ability. Technique can be coached. The existing Atlanta OL (Matthews, Lindstrom, Bergeron, Dalman group) has extensive reps together, so the priority may be developing a long-term replacement rather than an immediate starter.
OT.scheme_fit_narrative: We need an OT who can move his feet. This is a wide-zone offense — if our tackles can't reach-block and get lateral displacement, the run game dies. We also need guys who can transition from run blocking to pass protection on play-action seamlessly. Athletic tackles who can play in space are gold. Phone-booth-only maulers don't fit.

IOL.scheme_role: Interior OL in Stefanski's offense are critical to the wide-zone run game. Guards must execute combo blocks (double-teaming the DT before climbing to the LB), pull on counter and power concepts, and create lateral movement on stretch plays. The center must make line calls and handle nose tackles. Stefanski loves to pull his athletic guards — pulling guards on power, counter, and pin-and-pull perimeter concepts are a staple. Chris Lindstrom (All-Pro guard) is the model for what this offense demands.

Pass Protection.reasoning: Interior pass protection is necessary on dropback downs, particularly against interior DL who can collapse the pocket and disrupt the QB's footwork timing. Stefanski's play-action reduces overall dropback volume, but when the Falcons do pass traditionally, the IOL must hold up.

Run Blocking.reasoning: Wide-zone combo blocks and reach-blocking technique are the foundation. The guard must work in tandem with the center on double-teams, recognize when to climb to the second level, and sustain blocks through contact. The Browns finished top-6 in rushing yards every year with Callahan coaching the interior OL.

Pulling.reasoning: Stefanski loves to pull his guards — it's one of his signature concepts. Power, counter, pin-and-pull, and shotgun play-action with guard-pulling are all staples. The guard must be athletic enough to pull laterally, identify targets in space, and kick out or seal LBs. Joel Bitonio and Wyatt Teller were elite pulling guards in Cleveland.

Strength.reasoning: Baseline strength for sustaining blocks and handling interior DL. Wide-zone is more about leverage and angles than raw power, but the IOL must be strong enough to execute double-teams and manage bull rushers.

Anchor.reasoning: Must hold up against interior pass-rush penetration. Reduced importance because play-action reduces pure pass-rush exposure, but still necessary for the ~35-40% of plays that are dropback passes.

Versatility.reasoning: Stefanski values IOL who can play multiple positions. Callahan develops versatile linemen who can slide between guard and center as needed. Given injury risks and the importance of OL continuity, a guard who can play center (or vice versa) is extremely valuable in this scheme.

IOL.ideal_archetype: An athletic, pulling guard who excels in wide-zone combo blocks and can get to the second level in space. Must have the athleticism to pull on counter/power and the technique to sustain blocks in the run game. Think Joel Bitonio or Chris Lindstrom — guards who combine technical precision with athletic ability and can execute the diverse run-game menu that Stefanski and Callahan demand.

short_shuttle.reasoning: Pulling demands lateral agility. Guards who pull on counter/power must get to the POA quickly and adjust to moving targets. Athletic IOL make the pulling game function.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to handle double-teams and anchor against DTs, but athletic enough to pull. The ideal is in the 305-320 range.

bench_press.reasoning: Upper-body strength matters for sustaining blocks and controlling DTs at the POA. Not the primary trait but a useful indicator of play strength.

IOL.development_context: Callahan is the gold standard for IOL development. Bitonio, Teller, and Conklin all reached All-Pro levels under him. He can take a raw athlete with movement skills and teach zone-blocking technique. The current Falcons interior (Lindstrom, Bergeron, Dalman) provides a strong foundation. A young IOL prospect needs movement ability and coachability — Callahan will handle the rest.
IOL.scheme_fit_narrative: Our guards need to be athletes. They're going to pull on counter, pull on power, and execute wide-zone combos. We need guys who can move in space and get to the second level. Raw strength-only guards who can't pull don't fit this scheme. Give us a guard with good feet and a mean streak, and Callahan will make him a star.

EDGE.scheme_role: EDGE rushers in Ulbrich's defense are the primary pressure generators in a system that relies on four-man rushes and drops seven into coverage. They must win 1-on-1 against OTs consistently because the defense rarely blitzes to support them. Ulbrich deploys EDGEs in both hand-in-dirt DE alignments and standing up — James Pearce Jr. operates primarily as a speed-rush DE while Jalon Walker moves between EDGE and off-ball LB. The scheme demands high-motor, versatile rushers who can set the edge in the run game and generate consistent pressure. In 2025, Pearce (10.5 sacks) and Walker (5.5 sacks) combined for 16 sacks as rookies.

Pass Rush.reasoning: This is a front-four-pressure defense. The EDGE must win 1-on-1 rushes because Ulbrich's scheme provides minimal blitz support — at NYJ, the blitz rate was 16-17%. The EDGE's ability to generate pressure with only four rushers is the single most important defensive trait. Pearce's 14.7% pressure rate as a rookie demonstrates the standard.

Bend.reasoning: Speed-to-power is nice, but Ulbrich's top EDGEs (Pearce, Jermaine Johnson at NYJ) win with bend around the corner. The ability to flatten the arc and get to the QB while staying on balance is critical. Pearce's bend and flexibility were cited as his top traits coming out of Tennessee.

First Step.reasoning: In a four-man-rush system where pressure must come without help, the EDGE needs to win at the snap. A quick first step creates immediate stress on the OT and forces the QB to feel pressure before the rush even fully develops. With seven in coverage behind them, the EDGE gets time to finish — but he must threaten immediately.

Power.reasoning: Bull-rush and power-counter moves provide variety, but this defense prioritizes speed and bend over bull-rush. Power matters for setting the edge in the run game and converting speed to power at the apex of the rush. Not the primary trait but necessary for completeness.

Motor.reasoning: Ulbrich demands relentless effort and physicality. His 'violence' philosophy requires EDGEs who chase plays down from behind, finish every rep, and never take a play off. Motor also matters because the low blitz rate means the EDGE is rushing on every passing play — he can't take plays off.

Run Defense.reasoning: The EDGE must set the edge in the run game. Ulbrich's 2025 Falcons struggled with run defense (27th in EPA per rush allowed), so this is an area of growth. The EDGE can't be a liability against the run, but the primary value is as a pass rusher.

EDGE.ideal_archetype: A high-motor, bendy speed rusher who can win consistently off the edge with four-man pressure. Must have the versatility to align in multiple positions — standing up and hand-in-dirt. Ideally can occasionally drop into coverage on zone blitz looks (Walker's versatility is the ceiling). Think James Pearce Jr., Jermaine Johnson, or a young Chandler Jones — EDGEs who combine first-step explosion with bend and motor and can dominate without blitz help.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Speed off the edge is the primary weapon. Pearce ran 4.47 at the combine. The ability to threaten with pure speed forces OTs into oversets that open up inside counter moves.

height.reasoning: Long, rangy EDGEs who can convert speed to power at the apex of the rush. Length also helps set the edge in the run game.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to hold up at the POA against the run but light enough to maintain elite speed. Pearce (243) and Walker (245) represent the ideal range.

arm_length.reasoning: Length matters for hand placement on OTs and keeping blockers from getting into the body. Longer arms create leverage advantages in pass-rush and run-setting.

EDGE.development_context: Ulbrich has developed EDGEs at every stop. At UCLA, Anthony Barr led the nation in sacks. At NYJ, Jermaine Johnson made the Pro Bowl. In 2025 Atlanta, he developed two rookie EDGEs into immediate impact players. The staff (John Timu as OLB coach, Dave Huxtable as senior defensive assistant) has depth in pass-rush development. Raw athletes with speed and bend can be refined — pass-rush moves and counters can be taught. What must arrive is the physical tools (speed, bend, length). Run defense technique can be developed but must show baseline ability.
EDGE.scheme_fit_narrative: We need EDGEs who can win 1-on-1 because we're not going to blitz for them. The scheme drops seven into coverage, so the pressure has to come from the front four. Give us a guy who can bend the corner, has a motor that never quits, and can hold up against the run. Bonus points if he can stand up and drop into coverage occasionally — that's the versatility that makes this defense special.

DL.scheme_role: Interior DL in Ulbrich's defense serve a dual role: they must penetrate and create interior pressure on passing downs while holding up at the point of attack in the run game. The 3-technique DT is the primary interior disruptor — Ruke Orhorhoro ranked 3rd among DTs in QB pressure rate in 2025. The 1-tech/nose must control double-teams and occupy blockers to free up LBs. Ulbrich wants his DTs to be 'simple and violent' — strike, shed, and make plays. The front rotates frequently to keep rushers fresh.

Pass Rush.reasoning: Interior pressure is critical in a four-man-rush scheme. The DT must collapse the pocket to complement edge pressure. Quinnen Williams was elite in this role for the Jets. Orhorhoro provides this in Atlanta. Interior pressure forces the QB to step into the EDGEs.

Run Defense.reasoning: The Falcons ranked 27th in EPA per rush allowed in 2025 — run defense is a stated priority for improvement. The DT must control his gap, hold the point of attack against double-teams, and not get washed out of run lanes. This was a weakness Ulbrich explicitly wants to address.

First Step.reasoning: The DT must win his gap quickly to create disruption. In a low-blitz system, interior penetration must come from the DTs' own athleticism, not from overloading one side. A quick first step allows the DT to split double-teams before they can set.

Hand Usage.reasoning: Ulbrich's philosophy is 'simple and violent' — strike, shed, make plays. Elite hand usage (club-rip, swim, push-pull) allows the DT to control blockers and disengage to make plays. Quinnen Williams' hand technique was a model for this at NYJ.

Motor.reasoning: The defense's physicality-first identity requires DTs who play with relentless effort. Rotation keeps players fresh, but each snap must be played at maximum intensity. Ulbrich demands violence at the POA on every play.

Strength.reasoning: Baseline strength to hold up against double-teams and control the LOS. The 1-tech needs more strength (2-gapping/occupying) while the 3-tech can rely more on quickness. Both need enough strength to not get pushed off the ball in the run game.

DL.ideal_archetype: A disruptive, high-motor interior DL who can penetrate on passing downs and hold up against the run. The 3-tech should be an interior pass-rush specialist (think Quinnen Williams or Ruke Orhorhoro) with quick first step and violent hands. The 1-tech should be a space-eating nose who controls double-teams and frees up LBs (think a run-stuffing anchor). Both must play with Ulbrich's signature violence and effort.

weight.reasoning: The 3-tech needs quickness over size. The 1-tech needs mass to control the point of attack. Both need to fit within the rotational philosophy.

3_cone_drill.reasoning: Interior quickness is the 3-tech's bread and butter. Must be able to knife through gaps and redirect quickly. The 1-tech has less stringent agility demands.

bench_press.reasoning: Upper-body strength powers the hand usage that defines Ulbrich's DL play. Striking and shedding blockers requires functional pressing strength.

DL.development_context: Nate Ollie continues as DL coach, providing continuity. Orhorhoro's rapid development in 2025 (3rd among DTs in pressure rate) shows the staff can develop young DTs. Interior DL can be developed from raw athletes more effectively than EDGE because technique (hand usage, leverage) can be taught in a controlled space. What must arrive is the physical tools — quickness, explosiveness, functional strength.
DL.scheme_fit_narrative: Our DT needs to wreck the game from the inside. We're not sending five — we're trusting four to win. The 3-tech must be a disruptive force who can collapse the pocket and complement our EDGEs. The nose must eat double-teams and make the LBs' jobs easier. Both must play with violence and effort every snap.

LB.scheme_role: The off-ball LB in Ulbrich's defense is the field general — the defensive QB who sets calls, processes offensive formations, and must be reliable in both run fits and coverage. In nickel (~70% of snaps), there's typically only ONE off-ball LB on the field, meaning he must handle a massive workload. Ulbrich's LB history is extensive — he coached Deion Jones and De'Vondre Campbell to multiple 90+ tackle seasons, developed Quincy Williams into an All-Pro at NYJ, and was a 10-year NFL LB himself. Kaden Elliss and Troy Andersen represent the current ATL LB room; Elliss had a career year in 2025.

Tackling.reasoning: As potentially the only LB on the field in nickel, he must be a sure tackler who limits yards after contact. Ulbrich's LBs have consistently been volume tacklers (Deion Jones, Oluokun, Williams all posted 90+ tackle seasons). Missed tackles are catastrophic when there's no second LB behind you.

Coverage.reasoning: With man coverage rates of 30%+, the LB must cover TEs and RBs in man assignments. He also must read route combinations in zone and react to underneath threats. Ulbrich's low-blitz system keeps the LB in coverage on most passing downs. Quincy Williams was kept in coverage rather than blitzing because his coverage value was higher.

Pass Rush.reasoning: Ulbrich's low-blitz philosophy means the LB rarely rushes the passer. Occasional A-gap mugs and simulated pressures are used, but the LB's pass-rush value is limited. Walker handles the hybrid LB/rusher role — the MIKE LB stays in coverage. When blitzing, efficiency matters more than volume.

Instincts.reasoning: As the solo LB in nickel, he must diagnose run/pass quickly, identify blocking schemes, and trigger to the ball. Pattern-reading in zone coverage requires instinctual recognition of route combinations. Ulbrich values LBs who 'just know how to run the defense' (his description of C.J. Mosley).

Athleticism.reasoning: Must be athletic enough to cover slot WRs and athletic TEs in man coverage. Ulbrich's system asks LBs to match up with skill players in space. Sideline-to-sideline range matters when you're the lone LB — you have to cover a lot of ground.

Range.reasoning: Must be able to flow sideline-to-sideline in the run game and cover the width of the field in zone. Important but less critical than instincts and coverage because the DL is supposed to funnel plays into the LB's gap.

Block Shedding.reasoning: OL will climb to the second level and target the LB. He must disengage from blocks to make tackles. Secondary to coverage and instincts because the DL is designed to absorb blockers, but necessary for run defense.

Pre-Snap Diagnosis.reasoning: As the defensive play-caller, the LB must process offensive formations, identify personnel, set the defense, and adjust pre-snap. This is the quarterback of the defense and must be cerebral enough to handle the mental demands of calling Ulbrich's multiple fronts.

LB.ideal_archetype: A rangy, coverage-capable MIKE LB who can handle being the sole LB on the field in nickel packages. Must be a reliable tackler with the instincts to diagnose plays quickly and the athleticism to cover TEs and RBs in man. Think C.J. Mosley in his prime or Quincy Williams at his best — a field general who processes the game quickly and can cover ground in both run support and pass defense.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Must be fast enough to cover TEs and RBs in man and flow sideline-to-sideline. Ulbrich's best LBs (Jones 4.38, Williams 4.49, Kendricks 4.61) have all been plus athletes.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to take on blocks and set the edge but light enough to cover in space. The modern nickel-era LB is leaner than the old-school run-stuffers.

3_cone_drill.reasoning: Change of direction matters for covering routes and redirecting in zone. Athletic LBs who can mirror TEs' breaks are premium in Ulbrich's coverage-heavy scheme.

LB.development_context: Ulbrich is one of the NFL's best LB developers. Deion Jones, De'Vondre Campbell, Eric Kendricks, Anthony Barr, Quincy Williams — all produced at the highest level under him. Barrett Ruud (LBs coach) adds another development resource. Ulbrich can develop instincts and processing through his teaching methods. What must arrive is athleticism and coverage ability — you can't coach speed and range. Tackling technique and block-shedding can be refined.
LB.scheme_fit_narrative: We need a guy who can run the defense and cover ground. He's going to be the only LB out there in nickel 70% of the time, so he needs to be an athlete who can cover TEs and handle man assignments. He needs to be cerebral enough to make calls and process offensive formations. We'll develop his run-game technique — we need the speed and coverage instincts to arrive on Day 1.

CB.scheme_role: CBs in Ulbrich's defense must be capable in both man and zone coverage. The defense uses man coverage ~30% of the time (significantly more than most zone-heavy schemes), requiring CBs who can match up 1-on-1. In zone, they play Cover 3 (flat/deep thirds) and Cover 4 (quarters with pattern-matching). Ulbrich's CBs are tethered to one side of the field — they don't travel with receivers. A.J. Terrell is the elite CB1 who allows Ulbrich to roll coverages and blitz from the slot with confidence. The outside CBs play press at the LOS and must disrupt timing at the snap.

Man Coverage.reasoning: Ulbrich's defense uses man coverage at a top-10 rate in the NFL (~30-33% of snaps). The CB must be able to mirror routes, stay in phase, and handle 1-on-1 assignments without safety help on Cover 1 calls. Sauce Gardner's first-team All-Pro seasons in man coverage set the standard. A.J. Terrell's 1.2 yards of average separation allowed shows the expectation.

Ball Skills.reasoning: Ulbrich wants turnovers — it's central to his 'violence' philosophy. The CB must attack the ball at the catch point and create takeaways. However, Ulbrich's primary CB expectation is lockdown coverage, not interception hunting. Ball skills are secondary to coverage ability.

Zone Coverage.reasoning: Cover 3 and Cover 4 are the primary zone calls. The CB must read route combinations, carry vertical routes, and break on throws in his zone. Pattern-matching zone requires the CB to play man-like technique within zone rules, which demands both instincts and technical precision.

Speed.reasoning: Man coverage against NFL WRs demands recovery speed. The CB must have the ability to stay on top of routes and recover when beat off the line. Speed also matters for making plays on deep throws in Cover 3 where the CB must cover a deep third.

Press.reasoning: Ulbrich's CBs play press at the LOS to disrupt timing. Press technique is critical for man coverage success and for re-routing receivers in zone. Sauce Gardner's press technique was a cornerstone of the Jets' defense. Terrell's press disruption (1.2 yards average separation allowed) shows the demand.

Nickel.reasoning: The nickel CB plays ~70% of snaps and must handle slot WRs in both man and zone. Billy Bowman Jr. has filled this role capably in 2025 (86.3% of snaps as a rookie). The nickel must be physical enough to handle run fits from the slot and quick enough to cover slot routes.

CB.ideal_archetype: A press-man corner who can play on an island without safety help on Cover 1 calls. Must be technically sound in both man and zone, with the ability to disrupt at the line and mirror routes in phase. Recovery speed is essential for when he gets beat. Think Sauce Gardner or A.J. Terrell — physical, disciplined CBs who erase their side of the field and allow the defense to be aggressive elsewhere.

height.reasoning: Length matters for press technique and contesting catches at the catch point. Ulbrich's best CBs (Gardner 6-3, Terrell 6-1) have been above-average in height. The outside CB needs enough length to disrupt timing at the line.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Man coverage demands speed to stay on top of routes and recover from false steps. The CB must be fast enough to match NFL speed WRs without needing to cheat alignment.

weight.reasoning: Must be physical enough to press and tackle in run support. Ulbrich's emphasis on violence extends to CBs — they must be willing to support the run and make tackles in the open field.

CB.development_context: Ulbrich coached Sauce Gardner to first-team All-Pro in his first two seasons. Justin Hood (secondary coach) and Ricky Manning (assistant DBs/nickels) will handle CB development. The staff can develop zone technique and press refinement. What must arrive is the physical tools — speed, length, hip fluidity — and the competitive temperament to play man coverage. Ulbrich explicitly teaches the 'Peanut Punch' turnover technique, so ball skills can be developed. The defense demands CBs who are willing to tackle — that mentality must be present.
CB.scheme_fit_narrative: We need corners who can play man and not flinch. One-third of our defensive calls are man coverage — the corner is going to be on an island against the WR1 without safety help. He needs to press, disrupt timing, and stay in phase. He also needs to be physical in the run game because we're not pulling him out for a safety. Give us a corner with speed, length, and competitive fire.

S.scheme_role: Safeties in Ulbrich's defense serve distinct roles. The free safety (post safety) plays deep-middle responsibility in Cover 1 and Cover 3, reading the QB and breaking on the ball. The strong safety is more of a hybrid — used in the box, near the LOS, and as a robber/hook defender. Jessie Bates III is the rangy center-fielder type, while Xavier Watts (5 INTs as a rookie in 2025) showed elite ball skills. Ulbrich's defense asks safeties to support the run aggressively while maintaining deep coverage integrity. In man concepts, safeties may cover TEs or serve as the single-high free player.

Man Coverage.reasoning: Safeties cover TEs in man on Cover 1 calls. The strong safety especially must be able to match up with athletic TEs in man. Not the primary coverage tool (CBs handle WRs), but necessary on ~30% of snaps.

Range.reasoning: Cover 1 and Cover 3 both require a single-high safety with elite range. He must be able to patrol the deep middle and get to both sidelines. Jessie Bates III's range is the model — the defense only works if the post safety can cover enough ground to erase deep mistakes by CBs or LBs. Xavier Watts' immediate impact came partly from his range.

Ball Skills.reasoning: Ulbrich's turnover-focused philosophy makes ball skills premium for safeties. Xavier Watts had 5 INTs in 2025. The post safety sees the entire field and must create turnovers when the QB makes mistakes. Ball-hawking safeties are force multipliers in a defense that generates pressure with the front four.

Tackling.reasoning: Ulbrich demands physicality from everyone, including safeties. The safety is the last line of defense — missed tackles in the secondary are catastrophic. In run support, the safety must trigger downhill and make stops. Ulbrich's 'violence' philosophy extends to the safety position.

Speed.reasoning: Range requires speed. The post safety must be fast enough to get from the deep middle to the sideline on deep throws. The strong safety must be fast enough to cover TEs and close on runners in space. Both need above-average speed for the modern game.

Nickel.reasoning: In some sub-packages, the safety may play in nickel/box roles as a hybrid defender. Billy Bowman fills the primary nickel role, but a versatile safety who can play in the box adds defensive flexibility. Ulbrich used Jabrill Peppers-type hybrid safeties in some packages.

S.ideal_archetype: A rangy, ball-hawking center-fielder who can patrol the deep middle in Cover 1/Cover 3 and come down to make physical plays in run support. Must have the instincts to read QBs and create turnovers. Think Jessie Bates III or Xavier Watts — safeties who combine range with playmaking ability and aren't afraid to tackle. The strong safety should be a physical, box-capable player who can cover TEs and support the run from depth.

40_yard_dash.reasoning: Range in Cover 1/Cover 3 is speed-dependent. The post safety must be fast enough to cover the width and depth of the field. Watts ran 4.41 — that type of speed is the ideal.

height.reasoning: Length helps for contesting catches on deep shots and covering TEs in man. Not a hard threshold, but taller safeties have advantages in jump-ball situations.

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to tackle effectively in run support and handle physical assignments. Ulbrich's violence philosophy requires safeties who can deliver hits.

S.development_context: Justin Hood (secondary coach) oversees safety development. Ulbrich developed Xavier Watts into a Day 1 starter with 5 INTs in 2025 — showing the staff can develop young safeties quickly. Ball skills and instincts are the hardest to develop — those should ideally arrive. Tackling technique, coverage technique, and pre-snap recognition can all be refined. The scheme's clarity (clear safety responsibilities in Cover 1 and Cover 3) helps young safeties play fast.
S.scheme_fit_narrative: We need a safety who can cover ground and make plays on the ball. Our post safety is the last line of defense in Cover 1 — he has to have the range to get sideline to sideline and the ball skills to make the QB pay for mistakes. He also has to be a willing tackler because we play physical defense at every level. Give us a rangy athlete with great instincts and we'll put him in position to make plays.

scheme_draft_intelligence:
The Atlanta Falcons enter the 2026 draft in a unique position: a new head coach (Kevin Stefanski) inheriting a roster with significant offensive talent (Bijan Robinson, Drake London, Kyle Pitts, an experienced OL) but a defense still in Year 2 of a rebuild under retained DC Jeff Ulbrich. The coaching staff's scheme priorities will fundamentally shape their draft approach. On offense, Stefanski's wide-zone, play-action system aligns perfectly with the existing roster — Robinson is an elite zone runner, the OL has years of continuity, and Pitts slots into Stefanski's TE-heavy passing attack. The offensive draft priority will center on QB (Michael Penix Jr. is returning from a torn ACL, creating uncertainty) and potentially a second TE or a WR who can provide the vertical speed element that stretches the defense.

Defensively, the value differentials are enormous. Ulbrich's four-man-rush philosophy creates outsized value for interior DL who can generate pressure without blitz help — a trait many teams undervalue because they can scheme pressure through blitzes. A DT who wins 1-on-1 inside is worth more to Atlanta than to a blitz-heavy defense. Similarly, Ulbrich's man-heavy coverage philosophy (30%+ man rate) means press-man CBs have elevated value — teams running zone-only schemes won't value press technique as highly. The Falcons' biggest defensive need is run defense (27th in EPA per rush allowed in 2025), meaning interior DL who can control the point of attack while also rushing the passer are premium targets.

The coaching staff's development track record shapes where value can be found. Ulbrich's elite ability to develop EDGEs and LBs means the Falcons can take raw athletes at those positions and trust the development pipeline. Callahan's OL development pedigree means Atlanta can take a developmental OT or IOL without needing him to start immediately. Conversely, the TE position represents a scheme-driven hidden value: Stefanski and Rees have an unmatched track record of TE development (Njoku, Fannin, and now Pitts' career year). A Day 2 or Day 3 TE prospect who fits the athletic profile could become a featured weapon in this system in ways other teams wouldn't project.

Historically, Stefanski's teams have invested heavily in the trenches. In Cleveland, the Browns routinely prioritized OL and DL in early rounds. New GM Ian Cunningham has explicitly stated the Falcons will 'build the team through the trenches.' Combined with Callahan's hiring as the first offensive staff move, the signal is clear: expect significant draft capital devoted to OL and DL. The Falcons traded away their 2026 first-round pick for Pearce, so they'll be working without a first-rounder, making Day 2 efficiency critical. This is where Ulbrich's development ability creates value — finding second-round EDGEs, third-round LBs, and mid-round DBs who can be coached up within the system.

The biggest scheme-driven value differential for Atlanta may be at LB. Ulbrich's system demands an athletic, coverage-capable MIKE who can handle being the sole LB in nickel packages 70% of the time. Many teams can hide LBs in zone or use two-LB sets that reduce individual burden. In Atlanta's system, the MIKE must be a complete, three-down player. This elevates the value of athletic, coverage-first LBs who test well at the Combine but are undervalued by teams that don't ask as much of the position in coverage. If the Falcons can find a Quincy Williams-type athlete in the middle rounds, Ulbrich's development system could turn him into a cornerstone.
